summary: Gruber continues his critique of Google's stance on patent acquisitions, arguing that Google's pursuit of the Nortel patents was just as competitively motivated as Apple's or Microsoft's actions. He rejects the framing of Google's patent strategy as purely 'defensive,' contending that Google wanted the patents to strengthen its negotiating position against legitimate patent claims that Android violates. He dismisses Google's public complaints as hypocritical whining after losing a competitive bid, and argues that Microsoft and Apple are within their rights to assert patents against Android. The post culminates in a broader accusation that Google habitually copies competitors' work and then acts offended when they fight back.
key_points: Google's pursuit of Nortel patents was competitively motivated, not altruistic — they wanted leverage against existing patent claims Android violates
The distinction between 'offensive' and 'defensive' patent strategies is misleading; both serve competitive ends
Google's complaint isn't about playing the patent game, but about whining after losing the bid to competitors
The patent system may need reform, but companies must compete within existing laws using whatever advantages they have
Google has a pattern of copying competitors (iPhone UI, Yelp data, Facebook, Groupon) then acting aggrieved when those competitors push back
money_quotes: Google wanted this newly available pile of old patents to give itself a stronger negotiating hand regarding all the other existing patents that Android — purportedly — violates.
Google's blog post comes across as whining that Apple and Microsoft (Microsoft especially) aren't sitting back and allowing Android to destroy their businesses.
Maybe the patent system should be killed. It certainly should be reformed. But these are the laws we have.
Google's ace in this fight: search revenue. Microsoft's ace: its patent library. You fight with what you have.
What I'm complaining about isn't Google playing the game, but rather their insistent whining about their competitors only after they lost the game.
Google seems to feel entitled to copy whatever it wants.
And yet it's Google that acts as though it has been offended when these competitors fight back.
key_insight: Google's patent complaints are hypocritical because their bid was just as competitively motivated as their rivals', and their real grievance is simply that they lost.
